artifacts.content:
Subject: Catching you up on…well, everything

Ana,

I owe you a big catch‑up, so here goes while the coffee’s still hot.

School stuff first: Sofia scared the hell out of us last October with that three‑day suspension from East for vaping. You know how she is—so smart and also so easily swayed by what her friends are doing. I almost bailed on the last weekend of that DEI certificate series at the Denver Nonprofit Center because it landed the same week. In the end I just paid the $75 rescheduling fee so I could sit in on the meeting with her counselor, Ms. Jenkins, at East. Worth every penny to be there in person and not on Zoom.

Work has its own drama. At DRRA we’re still digging out from that $187,000 grant‑reporting mess we uncovered last May tied to Daniel Ortiz’s documentation. I’ve spent more time than I care to admit on the phone with the Colorado Office of Behavioral Health and our auditor from CliftonLarsonAllen, trying to finalize the corrective action plan and repayment schedule. It’s exhausting, but it’s also sharpening my bullshit detector when it comes to financial oversight.

All of that is layered on top of Papá’s health. I keep thinking back to his stroke at Denver Health in March 2015 and how I shelved the whole DU MSW idea so I could coordinate his rehab three evenings a week from the apartment here on Pine Ridge Court. I’m glad I did, but I feel that detour now when I look at my peers with advanced degrees.

Anyway, that’s my Sunday brain dump. How are your clinic shifts? Text me your schedule so we can line up a Cheesman Park walk soon.

Love,
Jon

Subject: Papá, probation, and this new re‑entry group idea

Miguel,

Been thinking about you a lot with all the overtime you’re pulling, so I figured I’d write instead of just sending another thumbs‑up text.

I had a long conversation with one of the guys in our re‑entry group at DRRA last week, and it brought me right back to that DUI I caught in Aurora on April 9, 2004—10 days in Arapahoe County Detention and the $3,800 fine I paid off over two years. I don’t talk about it much, but those court‑mandated classes and the whole probation maze are a big part of why I take this justice‑involved work so seriously.

It also reminded me of when I was still on unsupervised probation for the 2012 DUI and we did that one‑year, $4,500 vendor agreement between DRRA and the Denver intergroup office to bulk‑buy A.A. books for the drug court at the Denver Justice Center. I was paranoid enough that I emailed my probation liaison and even checked with Judge Harrison’s clerk just to be sure no one thought I was personally profiting from A.A. literature.

Now, with this new batch of guys coming out of county and community corrections, I’m trying to design support that feels like what helped me in 2004–2006—strong sponsorship and meetings—without blurring the line into being some kind of court program. I keep hearing Papá’s stories about that mess of a “treatment” setup he got stuck in down in El Paso in the late ’80s where they mixed A.A. and paid counseling and nobody was clear who was accountable.

Anyway, that’s my brain on a Tuesday night. How’s the house project going? Need me to come up and help Papá with that lawnmower again?

Abrazo,
Jon

metadata.subject: Papá, probation, and this new re‑entry group idea

artifacts.content:
Title: Service + work boundaries – stuff to unpack with sponsor

1. York Street / DRRA colleague (2012–2015)

I keep thinking about the guy from Capitol Hill Primary Purpose and DRRA I was informally sponsoring. When he relapsed in October 2014, came to work drunk, and HR had to terminate him, I spent several evenings—3–4 hours at a time—hauling him to late‑night meetings at the York Street Club and then sitting with him in detox at Denver Health. Need to look honestly at what was Twelfth Step work and what was me trying to rescue.

2. Mark L. / safety at home group (March 2021)

That nearly two‑hour group conscience at CHPP after Mark L. got served with the restraining order for alleged stalking at the First United Methodist Church basement really shook me. I facilitated, but part of me was still in “Delegate mode,” trying to model something for Area 10 instead of just being one home group member.

3. Mental health disclosure (July 2013 / August 2020)

I’m still uneasy about how much I shared at the West Central Regional Forum in August 2020 about that night in July 2013—sitting in the CHPP parking lot, calling Dr. Karen Patel because I didn’t trust my own thoughts, then driving straight to the emergency counseling session at Denver Mental Health Center. It helped people, but I don’t want to turn my suicidal ideation into a stock story.

Questions for next sponsor call:
- Where’s the line between appropriate vulnerability and emotional exhibitionism in service?
- How do I set clearer boundaries when work, A.A., and my own history collide?
- What am I still trying to prove?

metadata.title: Service + work boundaries – stuff to unpack with sponsor

artifacts.content:
Subject: Sofia, hospital meetings, and this DEI stuff

Elena,

I wanted to loop you in on a couple of things rather than try to cram it into a rushed hand‑off in the driveway.

First, I met again with Dr. Melissa Chang at the adolescent program over at Denver Children’s this week. It’s partly about Sofia’s history there—remember when her East High counselor referred her in Spring 2018 after that drinking episode at the party—but also because I’m lining up another round of A.A. CPC/PI presentations for their teens. It still feels weird walking into that building wearing both my “District 13 chair” hat (well, former chair now) and my “dad who’s scared for his kid” hat.

On the DEI front, I finally finished that certificate series at the Denver Nonprofit Center that got derailed when Sofia was suspended for vaping last October. Paying the $75 to move the final weekend so I could sit with you and Ms. Jenkins at East was the right call, even if my budget winced.

All of this is feeding into some bigger work stuff too. At DRRA we’re still working through the corrective action plan on that $187,000 reporting discrepancy tied to Daniel Ortiz that showed up in our internal review last May. Between calls with the Colorado Office of Behavioral Health and our auditor, I feel like half my life is spreadsheets and the other half is parenting crises.

Anyway, I appreciate how we handled the last parent‑teacher meeting together. Let’s try to keep that tone for whatever the next curveball is.

Jon

metadata.subject: Sofia, hospital meetings, and this DEI stuff

artifacts.content:
Title: Career detours + money stress – personal inventory

Education / training
- Fall 1999: Failed that 3‑credit statistics course at CU Boulder because I kept missing the Tuesday/Thursday lectures in Ketchum Hall to make the DUI education sessions at the Denver County Probation Office with Ms. Hernandez. I told myself I was “doing the responsible thing,” but the truth is I let everything get chaotic.

- January 2010: Started the Nonprofit Management Certificate at University of Denver right after the divorce with Elena was finalized in Denver County Court in December 2009. Had to ask Ms. Patel in the bursar’s office for a partial tuition deferment because I was still digging out from the $6,200 in legal fees and back child support. Embarrassing, but it kept me moving forward.

- 2015–2017: Put the MSW idea on hold after Papá’s stroke at Denver Health in March 2015. Spent three evenings a week from the apartment here at 742 Pine Ridge Court coordinating his post‑stroke rehab instead of studying for GREs.

Work / side gigs
- 2016–2019: Picked up that consulting contract with Dr. Hannah Klein at Denver Behavioral Health Associates on Colfax in March 2017 mainly to cover the extra $850/month temporary child support the judge ordered after we filed separation papers in August 2016. Good experience (outcomes tracking, integrating A.A. referrals), but I was running on fumes.

Questions:
- Where did I act out of fear vs. principle?
- How much of my current money anxiety is old court/tuition shame still echoing?

metadata.title: Career detours + money stress – personal inventory

artifacts.content:
Title: Weekly therapy + Marco – schedule block

Event: Marco – therapy w/ Dr. Nair (Children’s)
Date: Every Tuesday, 2:30–4:30 PM
Location: Children’s Hospital Colorado – Aurora
Attendees: Jonathan, Marco

Notes:
- Leave DRRA office by 2:30 PM to beat I‑225 traffic, 45 min cushion for parking and check‑in.
- Standing appointment with Dr. Priya Nair for Marco’s anxiety and school refusal that spiked after Elena and I finalized the divorce in January 2020.
- Between Feb 2020 and Oct 2021 I’ve informally cut back from 50–55 hour weeks at DRRA to more like 35–40 so I can do this consistently.
- Leadership team meeting permanently moved from Tuesdays 3:00 PM to Fridays 9:00 AM. Deputy director covering most evening community‑outreach events.

Related reminders:
- Follow up with Denver Family Court Services about any lingering paperwork from the September 2020–March 2021 custody modification. Keep practicing “restraint of tongue and pen” in emails with Elena.
- Block time Thursday mornings for my own work on panic/PTSD tools from the 12‑week trauma‑focused outpatient program I did at Denver Health Behavioral Health with Dr. Karen Liu back in Spring 2018. Those grounding skills helped a ton when I chaired that heated DEI listening session at the DoubleTree Denver Tech Center in June 2019—might be useful with Marco’s flare‑ups too.

Subject: Quick update before Sunday dinner

Mam á y Papá,

Just wanted to send a quick update before I drive up to Thornton on Sunday.

Work: At DRRA we’re still cleaning up old financial stuff. When I stepped into the Executive Director role in July 2019, I found that June 18, 2019 IRS letter about about $42,000 in unpaid payroll taxes from 2017–2018. Our CPA, Melissa Chang, and I have been going downtown to the IRS office on 19th Street and finally have a payment plan and stricter controls—dual signatures on any checks over $5,000, that kind of thing. Not fun, but it’s getting handled.

A.A.: I’ve been thinking a lot about safety and inclusion. You remember how upset I was after that blow‑up at the Galena Street Club on October 26, 2019 between Serenidad en Sobriedad and the Capitol Hill Young People’s Group—Spanish‑speaking newcomers getting mocked. I ended up facilitating three bilingual mediation meetings in November and December with Carlos Méndez, their GSR, and some of the young people. We drafted a joint amends statement and some simple guidelines about welcoming people regardless of language or age. It reminded me of why I still love A.A. service.

Family: The custody stuff with Elena finally settled back in March 2021 after all those Denver District Court hearings and co‑parenting classes at the Family Court Services office. It was hard, but I really did try to use A.A. principles—honesty, patience, restraint of tongue and pen. Sofia and Marco seem more relaxed with the new schedule.

I’ll bring pan dulce on Sunday. Let me know if you need anything from King Soopers.

Con cariño,
Jonathan
